title: Neural Signal Propagation
description: Watch an electrical signal travel down a neuron via action potentials — the language of the brain.

chain_length.label: Neuron Chain Length
chain_length.description: Number of neurons in the signal pathway

signal_strength.label: Signal Strength
signal_strength.description: Action potential threshold — below threshold, signal dies

narration.text: One neuron fires — an action potential. Sodium ions rush in, creating an electrical spike that travels down the axon at up to 120 metres per second.
The signal reaches the axon terminal. Neurotransmitters are released into the synapse — the gap between neurons.
Neurotransmitters bind to receptors on the next neuron. If enough bind, the next action potential fires. This is all-or-nothing — signals don't fade, they either fire or don't.
Notice neurons briefly turning grey after firing — the refractory period. Sodium channels are reset. The neuron cannot fire again for ~1-2 milliseconds, ensuring signals travel in one direction.
The signal has propagated through the entire chain. Your brain runs 86 billion of these simultaneously to process a single thought.
Anaesthetics block sodium channels — stopping action potentials from firing. Pain signals literally cannot reach the brain.
